item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good work on the skip, it works really well!

Although the presentation/execution of the notes was a bit standard, the thematics and execution of it was still super solid.

+1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the… story and "weirdness" of this one (if that's saying anything for an SCP). I don't have a reptilian fear, but the reptilian human fetus being removed from the iguana shocked and frightened me. That is the anomalous horrifying properties of SCPs I like to see.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat skip, I was reminded of Oscar Wilde's The Picture of Dorian Gray and H.P. Lovecraft's Pickman's Model. The experiment log and Cavendish's diary particularly stand out, and the whole article has this nice creepy vibe. I enjoyed the mental image of every reptile within a 5km radius converging on Site-11, and the idea of any reptiles, even turtles, developing venom glands if exposed to the painting.

+1

Also, is there a reason the painting is kept at -10°C? Is it just to help preserve it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd really like to see more of Jardine. Just because the Foundation knows of 4 paintings by him doesn't mean there aren't many more out there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This foetus was reptilian

This kinda bugged me, but otherwise, great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting, specially on the addenda. +1, good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why am I imagining this cross tested with 682? Bad idea, brain. Bad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Normally I'm not a huge fan of cross testing but I can't help but wonder how this skip with interact with SCP-088 (The Lizard King). This is an excellent article with a old school vibe. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love every aspect of this! I'm a reptile-lover, so I had to like it on principle.

But I kept reading, and every bit of it is executed so well. I love the journal entry especially. Expertly done. It got my vote immediately!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you overestimate the worldwide distribution of reptiles. There are places that this could be stored without attracting many reptiles. Anyway, there's no information in the article about the location of SCP-2071's containment chamber, because if you're guarding it, you're probably already on-site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reptiles within a certain distance of SCP-2071 will attempt to reach it by any means available to them. … is believed to extend at least 5 kilometres around the painting at all times. Reptiles affected by SCP-2071 will attempt to travel its location even at the risk of injury.

Wouldn't this require it to be kept somewhere in Antarctica? Otherwise the site the site building would be constantly surrounded by reptiles that would try to slip in whenever a door was opened, and the surrounding region would be depopulated of reptiles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, the literary and artistic references, the hoary title, the moment of queasiness because of the foetus, well done. I haven't upvoted just yet because I also felt there was something missing. I want to know about those other paintings! If no one has written them yet then I'll put that on a to-do list to at least think up suggestions.

Also, I really really want to know more about Cavendish. Maybe some backstory about the man further explaining why this portrait of him makes sense? Maybe the capture of an SCP resulting from a pregnancy of the painting? I just like the idea of this man coming back and still being mostly a man. Maybe a female researcher who had touched the painting could become pregnant, or the Foundation could allow a reptile pregnancy to come to fruition just to see what would happen.

By the way, Rioghail, are you a Hellboy fan? This reminded me of one of those little Mignolia vignettes that are always being seen in haunted castles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only one foetus would develop in that case, but Feccini is very cautious about any unexpected reactions from tests, and he's not likely to okay a second test until all the data from the first test has been completely sifted through and the research director can make a compelling argument for that testing will yield useful information.

It's a shame that you can't articulate what your issue with the piece is, but thanks for posting anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the story behind this one. It would make a great short story on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An excellent article. I kept feeling like a feel when I read Edgar Allan Poe. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I keep hitting +1, but it only went up once.

Anyway, I've always been a sucker for haunted paintings, and this just knocked that concept out of the park. You also managed to put in a twist that was subtle enough that it didn't beat me over the head, but not so subtle I missed it the first time around. I haven't liked a mainlister this much in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I am the King of Serpents, as that bastard said I was, why shouldn't I do the same?

Ohhhhh……

I like what you implied there. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't really care for the notes, they seemed to follow most of the tropes that "older notes" on this site tend to follow, at least in the writing style, but I did like the snake king portrait that preceded the notes. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"If we exposed multiple reptiles to the painting at the same time, would the same fetus (which I'm assuming is Cavendish) develop in both?" - is something I would ask myself if I were in any position to, or perhaps whether the fetuses differ depending on the species of reptile bearing them. I would quickly dismiss the thought of having any child be delivered though, since I can't quite see that not leading up to [DATA EXPUNGED].

Overall, this SCP didn't grab me, though, and I hate that I can't quite put my finger on why that is; perhaps because I only found the addendum and characters involved interesting, while getting to them was relatively boring. I'm edging more towards a neutral vote for now, but that might change, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, the entire thing was extremely well written, up to the ending. You executed your piece quite nicely, especially with the suicide note and the fetus. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this by scrolling through the Series III list and this one stuck out like a sore thumb (mostly because of how huge the fucking title is). Whatever, I still like it and you earned an upvote. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Another good SCP. I like the implications of this, though a bit more of the character of Cavendish would be nice.

Out of curiosity, do you know what the other paintings do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I haven't posted anything in ages (it's good to feel those post-related butterflies-in-the-stomach, I have to say), but I thought this was good enough to post. Hopefully I'm not wrong. This one is fueled by a trip round an art gallery, a poet, and too many Victorian novels.
